speaker: interviewer
text: What was the village?

speaker: interviewee
text: Copenhagen, New York--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, yeah. That's right

speaker: interviewee
text: --between Watertown and Lowville. I just heard from somebody. He said Watertown is 24 to 30 inches of snow. But in Copenhagen I had 20 feet of snow. This was the snow world. And as I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that where they had ski-- a ski resort there in Watertown?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, there was skiing resort.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And as I told you, when I-- I was doing house calls at night, I called the state police. And they were sending me right away two snowplows-- one in front of me, a [? turbo, ?] a rotator, and I was with the blades. The whole night-- it took a whole night. And here she was standing in the morning and waiting for me, because I did deliveries at-- in the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, you did?

speaker: interviewee
text: --at home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Today is December 11, 1995. The survivor is David Jakubowski, spelled, Jakubowski, J-A-K-U-B-O-W-S-K-I. My name is Marvin Greenberg. We are in Riviera Beach, Florida, United States. The language is English. And this is tape number 1. Today is December 11, 1995. My name is Marvin Greenberg, and I will be interviewing David Jakubowski, J-A-K-U-B-O-W-S-K-I. And we are in Riviera Beach, Florida, USA.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good morning, Dr. Jakubowski. Good morning, sir.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please tell us where and when you were born after you give your name and spell it. And then tell us something about your hometown as you were growing up and what was happening there.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Dr. David Jakubowski. I was born November 30, 1911 in Aleksandrow Kujawski. At that time, Poland was divided between three big occupation powers.

speaker: interviewer
text: So our town was under the Russians. I mention it for the reason that when the war broke out, the First World War in 1914, the Germans came from Pomerania, which belonged to-- to Germany. And in the first year of the war, they came to Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was a young kid. I remember it very well. I remember the officers, the German officers, which, at that time, they were really gentlemen, because I been always comparing these officers with the future Nazi officers. And they were in Poland. I see some of them, even the faces, and I remember one of them, the name, because he was flirting with my aunt. And-- and the war ended in 1918 in the Peace of Versailles. And Poland, part of Poland, or just like it was before 1792 when it was divided, because-- became a country, a state. My town, Aleksandrow Kujawski, was a small town. But there were several gymnasias. There was one gymnasium-- it was a school for the men and one for ladies. My school was run by the order-- Catholic order of the Silesian priests. Now, there were only three Jews in the whole school, and I must say we been treated very well there. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was-- until the end, which is the eighth grade, I was at top of the class. And in the eighth grade-- this was the last year of high school-- we were 12 of us. I was top in the class, and all 12 of us went to the universities. I remember one peculiar moment after the-- what we called the matura. This is the last [INAUDIBLE]. And I went to say good-bye to the principal, who was a priest, and I kneed before him, and he blessed me. And he asked me what my goal is. And I said, I wanted medicine. [INAUDIBLE] says, oh, if you go to Krakow, would be very simple. You just go to our Silesian order, but you have to convert to [? Catholicism. ?] I say, thank you, I'll try my best to do without it. Now, the life in-- in Aleksandrow Kujawski, this was a small town. There were Jews, and there were Poles. I didn't feel that anti-Semitism so much as it was in the other cities of Poland. I can only remember-- can never forget it. I can't get rid of it from-- from my mind. It was in 1920, after the war with the Bolsheviks, the army of General Haller-- part of it-- came to Aleksandrow. And the first thing what they did, they caught all the Jews with beards and cut them off in the streets. And then they went to houses, and whoever had a Jew-- whoever had a beard, they cut it right off. And this something which I never forget.

speaker: interviewer
text: What army was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was the Polish Army. General Haller was a famous general in the Polish Army. And you know, there was-- after the First World War, the Bolsheviks attacked Poland. So this was during the war with the Bolsheviks, and the Bolshevik lost it because of our Marshal Pilsudski.

speaker: interviewer
text: So as I said, Aleksandrow was a small town. As far as the Jews, they were mostly not Orthodox, but, you know, I don't know how to call them, not Conservative neither, because it didn't exist such a thing. Let's say-- my grandfather was a rabbi in a small town, a small village rather, north of Aleksandrow.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we had the shul, the synagogue, where my father attended. This was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it wasn't an Orthodox, but men were separated and the women were separated. They were upstairs sitting. And I can remember it very well, because the seat was hereditary, and we were next to the rabbi.

speaker: interviewer
text: So why I mention this, because when I was a young kid-- certain things I remember, and I can still hear it-- even on Yom Kippur when the cantor was praying, singing the song 'who will live and who would die,' there was one word he screamed so loud-- [NON-ENGLISH]-- that I still hear it. I can still hear it very well.

speaker: interviewee
text: And therefore, I told it to my rabbi, and every-- in-- in Syracuse, where we live now-- and every Yom Kippur I open the ark when the cantor prays this. Not only in Syracuse, but when I went to visit my daughter, the rabbi did the same thing there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was uneventful-- uneventful eight years, you know? We never felt anything. But as I mentioned, we are three ones-- three of us. We didn't feel that anti-Semitism which was felt almost all over Europe. We didn't feel it as much in Aleksandrow. It's a small town. Everybody knew each other. There was no special social life, because the Jews kept to themself. And the Poles-- I-- I don't know how to say Poles or Catholics-- kept to themself. Why I am saying I don't know to say Pole or not Pole, because they ask me, what are you? Pole? Because I was born in Poland. Or a Jew? It was a difficult answer. So I graduated from school, as I said, top of the class in 1929. What did your father do?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was a businessman. We had a store. And we lived for several years in Aleksandrow. And my sister was born-- was five years younger than I am-- four years younger. She was born in Aleksandrow too.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of store did he have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Food, you know, selling food and-- I don't know--

speaker: interviewer
text: Grocery store?

speaker: interviewee
text: Grocery store, big grocery store. We were doing all right. But then, we were not far for-- from a town, kurort, was called Ciechocinek. And we moved there for a few years, and that's-- there I was bar mitzvah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell that town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ciechocinek? C-I-E-C-H-O-C-I-N-E-K. This was a big kurort town. People were coming from all over Poland, especially from Warsaw, especially those who were-- who had severe or advanced arthritis.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned the word kuro--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, kurort.

speaker: interviewer
text: What does that mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kurort, it means-- I don't know the word in English-- treatment-- treatment or treatment place.

speaker: interviewer
text: A spa like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because there are-- spa like. They were taking baths, you know, salt baths and mud baths. And it was very good and very famous in Poland. We were-- there we had a different story again. Mostly-- and we were there-- we kept a place in Aleksandrow. We kept-- we went to Ciechocinek only for the summer, because the people were coming only in the summer there. There we have a little different story. Different-- and I don't how to say [NON-ENGLISH]. And in the beginning of 1930s, my parents moved to [NON-ENGLISH], where that's a big city in Pomerania of 2-- 300,000 people. Beautiful city. But I was at university. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Interesting to say that, you know, very few young men were taking into the armed services-- Jews-- into the armed services in Poland. Why they took me, I don't know. They took me into the officers school, which was not far from Posen, P-O-S-E-N. This was a one-year school. Again, we were only three Jews. We're all from-- from universities. I was from the University of Krakow. One was a history student from Warsaw, and one was from Lvov. Again-- and the rest-- there were a few hundred of them-- were mostly students of teachers school. It wasn't a university. They prepared them to be teachers under this-- some lower grades. Now again, I have to say-- not that I want to say something very good about me-- but I was, again, top. Because the three of us, you know, we were all university students. And we were top in the-- in to the officers schools. And from the officers schools, I was sent to Wloclawek, W-L-O-C-L-A-W-E-K, to the 14th Regiment of Infantry. But I was there as a medical officer. And all together it was one year, then they let us go. Every few years we had maneuvers. And when I was studying in Krakow medicine, I lived in the academic house. It was only for student-- Jewish students-- in a certain part of Krakow not far from the Vistula River.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is a time of my life which is unforgettable. Why? Because the anti-Semitism among the students was so high that every fall we went through the whole thing. We called this the fall maneuvers.

speaker: interviewee
text: A group of students-- they called them the Endeks, E-N-D-E-K-S, a very conservative group-- they were fighting with us. Not fighting with us, they were fighting us. Students coming out from different lectures were cut by razor blades attached to sticks. And this was going on for-- always for several weeks. And through the years, we started to call it the autumn maneuvers. It never change. Some of the professors were very nice. They stop lectures. Some continued. Now, on the lectures, during the years in university, which is hard for me to forget neither, they wanted us, the Endeks, wanted us to sit on the left side. There was huge amphitheater rooms for a few hundred students. I remember mostly the fourth, fifth, and sixth-year students were always together. And they wanted us to sit on the left side.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Jews-- this.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jews. Naturally, we didn't. So it was empty. The right and the middle was full, because there were a few hundred. The left was completely empty, and we were standing all up. Through all my clinical years-- four, fifth, and six-- we were standing up. We never sat down.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just the Jews stood up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because there were no seats?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I said they wanted us to sit on the left side of the room, and we didn't want to, only the Jews. And we were only 45, 50, and the other side were a few hundred. So we didn't want to sit down on the left side.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you stand?

speaker: interviewee
text: So up, on top of the room, because it was like an amphitheater.

speaker: interviewer
text: There were no seats there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. We were standing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ah.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember one professor-- professor of psychiatry-- was a very nice one. When they started to do something and trying to push us to the left side, he interrupted immediately-- the class is for, I think, for two or three weeks-- until this quiet-- quieted down, and then he started classes again. But this happened only with one professor. The rest, they didn't want to get mixed in. So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] this was happening every year.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any Jewish professors?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish professors? I don't remember not one even on medicine. It was-- it was a [? docent, ?] one [? docent, ?] assistant professor, was a Jew. I heard after the war he became the professor of internal medicine. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Now, the years went by. In 1939, I was very active in the academic life, very active.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were also in sports, weren't you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, in sports in school. But it was running, mostly running, 100 meters. There were 100 meters, 200 meters, 400 meters, and play tennis. Everybody did it in Poland or did something else. Now, I belonged-- during my childhood I belonged to-- when I was a young boy, 11, 10, 11, 12 years-- I belonged to a group, a Zionist group by the name of Trumpeldor group. He was a Jabotinsky follower. Jabotinsky was the one who started a movement in the Zionist-- Zionist movement, but a special movement. And Trumpeldor was his follower. Was known because he was in the army in Israel, you know, and he was killed there in 1919, I think. For several years I was there. And then I joined in Poland-- it's a general organization movement, which was run in Poland by a member of the Polish Sejm. Sejm was like here the House of Representative. Was-- his name was Greenberg, a very famous man in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of organization was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was the Zionist-- the general Zionist organization.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: General?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, sane.

speaker: interviewee
text: Or Zionist?

speaker: interviewer
text: Sane.

speaker: interviewee
text: Z-- huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: Sane? You said sane?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sejm, in Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: Just like the House of Representatives here. S-E-J-M. There was a senate, too. There were a few Jewish senators, too, there. But generally, you must say that in Poland was very anti-Semitic, one of the most-- behind-- just almost behind Russia. And it was the most anti-Semitic countries in whole Europe. I, personally, must say I didn't feel much. First of all, I was very blonde, light blonde. I didn't look like a-- didn't look like a Jewish boy. So I went over and were-- everywhere. And in 1939-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] as I said, I was in the army, yeah. In 1939, I went on a vacation with a academic group. I was very active, as I said, in academic life. And we were in a small, again, like a spa. And in August, I received-- received my papers. There was general mobilization, prepare-- preparing ourselves for the war against the Germans-- Hitler. Now, Hitler took over Germany in 1933. He became the chancellor after Hindenburg. In Germany, the life for the Jews was horrible, because--

speaker: interviewer
text: And so you received your papers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I went to my regiment.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in Wloclawek. This was the 4th Infantry Division. And from there, from Wloclawek, we went-- our division with other divisions-- towards the East Prussian border of Germany. And we were staying there-- this was the second half-- second half of August 1939. We were staying there in the woods, very close to the border of East Prussia. September 1, 1939, at 3 o'clock in the morning, we received artillery fire, very heavy artillery fire, from the German side. The war started. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We found a lot of spies at that time. For example, the man who was on the railroad station was giving different information to the German. He was a German. Because all Pomerania, there were a lot of Germans, because they belonged to Germany, as I mentioned before-- before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were under heavy artillery fire for almost two days. And after two days, we start to move back, the whole army. And we were moving mostly during the night, because during the day, as I can remember, the Germans were-- the German-- they call it Luftwaffe, the German Air Force-- was attacking us tremendously.

speaker: interviewee
text: They had fantastic planes, not their owns, the Czechoslovakian planes, the Stukas. And they were attacking us. One day-- I remember it very well too-- I almost got killed. The last second almost, I got into a deep-- into a hole, and the planes were over our heads.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we kept moving because they were attacking us. We didn't have much. We even didn't have the big tanks in our army-- small tankettes, and they came with these big Tiger tanks. So we didn't have any chance. And we're moving back until we came to Kutno. Now, Kutno was south of Warsaw. It was not far from Wloclawek.

speaker: interviewee
text: Why I mention this, because my parents had to run away from [NON-ENGLISH] because the Germans occupy there, and they knew that they would get into big trouble under the Germans, under the Hitler. So I went from Kutno to see them in Wloclawek, and that's the last time, in 1939, that I saw my parents and my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- oh, I remember the night of September 18 or 19. We were surrounded by the Germans. How did we know? Because there were fires all over around us. So September 19, we surrendered. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, we had-- I never forget this-- we had fantastic revolvers, and the Germans were after this. So the first thing when we came out, they ask if there are revolvers. But we buried all the revolvers.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they took us in a big transport to-- by train-- to city of Lodz in Poland. Now, there was a big hospital for POWs, and I was working in the hospital for the POWs from September until March 1940.

speaker: interviewee
text: In March 1940, they send us, the group of physician, to a small town near Posen in Pomerania. The name of it was [NON-ENGLISH]. Again there, there was a big hospital for the POWs, and I was working there [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] until June 1940.

speaker: interviewer
text: Something peculiar happened there in June 1940. According to the Geneva Convention, all the physician which are not needed for the treatment of the POWs should have been discharged. So they let us go, 11 of us. Why? I don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I remember the chief doctor was an officer from the First World War, a doctor, too. And he was telling me, as long as you can stay with us, which the military do, because when you go out, you will be under the SS and the Gestapo.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was a German military--

speaker: interviewee
text: The German-- yeah, German military man, very nice guy. I even have a document signed by him. Now-- That's it. I finish the sentence.
